title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who radically changed their eating habits. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Fueling Change: A New Approach to Food

texts.B:
### Julian, The Raw Food Experiment
'I was a professional chef, so cutting out heat was a major mental hurdle,' Julian admits. [47]Initially, the sheer volume of preparation required for every meal was quite a shock to my system.[/47] [53]I had to learn that flavor isn't always about complexity; sometimes it's about the integrity of the raw ingredient.[/53] [51]The most difficult part was the social isolation; friends stopped inviting me to dinner because they didn't know what to serve.[/51] [48]I was amazed to find that my chronic joint inflammation completely vanished after a month.[/48] [50]I realized that my culinary background helped me create textures that made the transition much more palatable for my family.[/50] It was a profound internal reset.
texts.A:
### Clara, The Sugar-Free Year
Clara decided to eliminate all processed sugars. 'I was a self-confessed addict,' she says. [55]The first two weeks were filled with intense mood swings and headaches, which I hadn't fully braced myself for.[/55] [49]The experience taught me that most of my 'hunger' was actually just emotional boredom or thirst.[/49] [56]If I hadn't joined an online support group, I definitely would have reached for a chocolate bar during the first crisis.[/56] [48]I noticed a significant improvement in my dental health, which my dentist described as a minor miracle.[/48] [52]I hadn't anticipated how much my sense of taste would sharpen; a simple apple now tastes like a decadent dessert.[/52] It’s been an eye-opening journey.
texts.C:
### Sam, The Intermittent Fasting Routine
'I wanted to regain control over my relationship with food,' Sam explains. [51]The biggest struggle was the cultural pressure to eat breakfast, which felt like an uphill battle at the office.[/51] [54]What kept me going was the immediate increase in my mental focus during the morning hours, which was a revelation.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the physical sensation of hunger to become so manageable after just a few days of consistency.[/47] [50]I found that the discipline required for fasting made me much more decisive in my job as a financial analyst.[/50] [53]I realized that we often eat out of habit rather than necessity.[/53] [49]It has simplified my life in ways I never imagined, saving both time and mental energy.
texts.D:
### Elena, The Local-Only Diet
Elena committed to eating only food grown within fifty miles. 'It’s harder than it sounds,' she laughs. [52]I hadn't planned for the seasonal monotony; eating root vegetables for three months was a test of my creativity.[/52] [54]I was moved by the relationships I built with local farmers, who taught me so much about soil health.[/54] [56]I wouldn't have known where to start if my neighbor hadn't shared her directory of local producers.[/56] [48]I noticed that my seasonal allergies practically disappeared, which was a benefit I hadn't even considered.[/48] [55]I found the initial research into every single ingredient incredibly time-consuming and frustrating.[/55] [49]It changed my perspective on global supply chains; I now value transparency over convenience.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by how their body adapted to a specific physical demand?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) was shocked by the volume of prep, and Sam (C) hadn't expected hunger to become manageable so quickly.

questions.question: notes an unexpected health benefit that they had not specifically sought?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions joint inflammation, Clara (B) dental health, and Elena (D) mentions her allergies disappearing.

questions.question: expresses a shift in their understanding of the purpose of eating or purchasing food?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) realized hunger was often emotional, Sam (C) realized we eat out of habit, and Elena (D) values transparency.

questions.question: mentions how their professional skills or traits were beneficial or influenced by the change?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) used his chef background, and Sam (C) became more decisive in his job as an analyst.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to social or cultural expectations?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) mentions social isolation, and Sam (C) mentions the pressure to eat breakfast at the office.

questions.question: admits to being unprepared for a specific sensory or variety-related issue?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) hadn't anticipated her sense of taste sharpening, and Elena (D) hadn't planned for seasonal monotony.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about simplicity or quality?
questions.explanation: Julian (A) learned about the integrity of raw ingredients, and Sam (C) realized food is often a habit.

questions.question: highlights the importance of the new connections or mental states they achieved?
questions.explanation: Sam (C) highlights his increased mental focus, and Elena (D) highlights the relationships with farmers.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or physical difficulty at the beginning of the process?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) had mood swings and headaches, and Elena (D) found the initial research frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges an external source of support that prevented them from giving up?
questions.explanation: Clara (B) credits her online support group, and Elena (D) credits her neighbor's directory.
